tawḥīd (prophetic vs kalām).key_passage: The path to [prophetic tawḥīd] was abandoned and eventually effaced only because the general public turned away from it, inclining instead toward the rhetorical embellishments of kalām theology... they assumed that this was the ultimate goal in drawing near to God—whereas in truth, that path is the furthest one from God and leads only to misguidance. Those who continued to walk the path of the messengers' tawḥīd came to be known as Ṣūfīs and ʿārifīn.
tawḥīd (prophetic vs kalām).key_point: Al-Tijānī distinguishes prophetic tawḥīd (lived, experiential) from kalām tawḥīd (rational-speculative). The former is what all messengers taught; the latter is a philosophical deviation. The Sufis preserve the prophetic tawḥīd.
tawḥīd (prophetic vs kalām).source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ, pp. 66–67

maʿrifa (giveness/encounter).key_passage: Maʿrifa is thus not only a matter of recognizing the oneness of the Real in a metaphysical sense, but relating to the Real in every circumstance, moment, givenness, and encounter. The Tijānī preference for the term maẓāhir (sites of manifestation) over tajalliyāt (disclosures) reflects a metaphysics grounded in encounter rather than transcendence.
maʿrifa (giveness/encounter).key_point: Tijānī maʿrifa is relational and immediate — God is encountered in the world as maẓhar (site of manifestation), not transcended. This is the defining distinction from the Akbarian-Sufi model.
maʿrifa (giveness/encounter).source: AK's notes on the Tijānī tradition

fanāʾ (annihilation of annihilation).key_passage: There is a time when the knower (ʿārif) undergoes annihilation (fanāʾ) and immersion (istighrāq), until he exits the sphere of his senses... This may sometimes be in the Essence of the Truth, where He manifests to him from His divine holiness some of His secrets, overflowing upon him, necessitating that he witnesses his own essence as identical with the Essence of the Truth due to his dissolution in it.
fanāʾ (annihilation of annihilation).key_point: Al-Tijānī on the ecstatic utterances (shaṭaḥāt) of the saints: fanāʾ in the Essence may cause the wali to speak with the tongue of the Truth, not his own. This is the proper hermeneutical key to such statements.
fanāʾ (annihilation of annihilation).source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ

ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya (Tijānī).key_passage: The essence (al-ʿayn) of relative oneness (wāḥidiyya) is the Muhammadan Reality (al-ḥaqīqa al-muḥammadiyya). To be drowned in it is to view it without veils.
ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya (Tijānī).key_point: Al-Tijānī identifies the Muhammadan Reality with the ʿayn of wāḥidiyya — the first locus of divine self-disclosure through which the divine Names and Attributes become accessible.
ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya (Tijānī).source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ

ranks of the hereafter.key_passage: Creation in the Hereafter consists of three categories: those who are the share of Allah's riḍā (the ṣiddīqūn, the aqṭāb, the prophets, and the messengers); those who are the share of His raḥma (the awliyāʾ, the righteous, and the martyrs); and those who are the people of pardon and forgiveness (the disobedient believers). And a fourth: khāṣṣat al-khāṣṣa — He loves them and they love Him (Q. 5:54).
ranks of the hereafter.key_point: Al-Tijānī's eschatological hierarchy: four levels, the highest being mutual love (yūḥibbuhum wa-yuḥibbūnahu) — the most elect, encompassing the ṣiddīqūn, aqṭāb, prophets, and messengers.
ranks of the hereafter.source: Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī (d. 1815), as reported in Muḥammad al-Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ
